title: Gemini 3.5 Pro enters second July week in preview, no GA date confirmed
subtitle: Google's flagship frontier model — with a 2M-token context window and a Deep Think reasoning mode — remains in limited preview after missing both its May and June GA targets, with a rumored July 17 launch window.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Gemini 3.5 Pro is entering the second week of July still in limited preview. Google has not published benchmarks, final pricing, or a general availability date. Reporting points to a July 17 target, and Google Cloud partners have been shown a two-million-token context window — the largest of any production frontier model, twice Fable 5 and Opus 4.8. A Deep Think reasoning mode that trades latency for accuracy on hard problems is expected to ship alongside. Preview pricing to enterprise testers has been described as roughly $1.25 per million input tokens and $10 per million output for the standard tier, rising to $2.50 and $15 above 200,000 tokens.

The interesting number is not the context window — it is the slip. Gemini 3.5 Pro missed a May GA target, then missed a June GA target, and now enters its third scheduled window with the same status. Google is known internally to have paused the launch for an architectural rebuild after early testers reported degradation on long-context recall. The context window is a leaderboard bragging right only if the model can actually use it — and calibrating recall across two million tokens is where the past year's delays across every frontier lab have concentrated.

The competitive frame matters. Anthropic just brought Fable 5 back and is metering it. OpenAI restricted GPT-5.6 Sol to select partners. Google entering GA with a cheaper, longer-context model would reset enterprise procurement conversations that have run in Anthropic's favor since May. A slipping launch keeps that reset on hold. Every week that Gemini 3.5 Pro stays in preview is a week Anthropic and OpenAI can continue to sign enterprise contracts against a Google flagship that officially does not yet exist.

A takeaway for learners: when a major AI product misses a launch window, the informative question is not 'when will it ship' but 'what is the blocker.' Long-context calibration, multi-turn tool use, and evaluator agreement on hard reasoning are all the current bottleneck classes. Reading the slip patterns is how you learn where the frontier actually is — the marketing dates tell you what companies wish were true, and the delays tell you where the engineering work still lives.

sources.title: Gemini 3.5 Pro Is Still in Preview Entering the Second Week of July — MarketScale
